Bearing 315

The Newsletter of Orienteering in the Pacific Northwest | March, 1996
Cascade Orienteering

Spring/Summer Preview

Yes, the Winter Series did come to a close only a few weeks ago, but the spring and summer main season will be upon us before you know it. Although there are no A-class events in Washington State this year, there are lots of 2-days in Washington and BC (including a stretch of five 2-day events on five consecutive weekends!), plus two major multi-days in the US. I won't give a detailed description of everything, but here's a brief rundown of what you can expect.

If you've never been to a two-day or multi-day, the camaraderie after the day's event is done, sitting around the campfire (or bar), arguing over the map, is a huge part of what makes these events so much fun. No matter what your skill level, try a 2-day or multi-day this summer, and see what real orienteering is like.

Many events require pre-registration, so plan ahead. There are probably several locals attending each one of these events, so call your club officers, or e-mail orienteering@u.washington.edu and you can surely find someone to share a ride and/or accommodations with. Here's the run-down of what's planned:

  • April 27/28. Two-day B in the Walla Walla/Richmond area. Sacajawea's first two-day event.
  • May 18/19. Sage Stomp A-event in Kamloops.
  • May 25/26. Island B-event at Newcastle Island.
  • June 1/2. Cle Elum Ridge. Probably one long day and a short day or relay.
  • June 8/9. Island B-event at Saltspring Island.
  • June 15/16. Bullfrog Flats two-day in Cle Elum. No word on the format yet.
  • June 29/30. Sage ROGAINE. I believe this is a full 24-hour ROGAINE (extended backcountry orienteering; 2-person teams).
  • July 4-14. Colorado 7-day. Confusingly, this has 8 days of orienteering this year. A premier event on first-class maps, with a friendly, low-key atmosphere. Most people camp out for the week.
  • July 20/21. Inland Empire Cup, Riverside State Park, Spokane. The legend continues! Fun, fast running, always superbly organized, and a gorgeous campground (with showers) right on the map. It just doesn't get any better than this.
  • July 26-28. BC Summer Games. I don't know if this is open to non-Canadians.
  • August 10-11. Bullfrog Flats "B". Another "B" event on this fine pair of maps.
  • August 10-25. The premier North American meet of the year for sure. Actually two events; a Canadian five-day in Ontario/Quebec, followed by a U.S. six-day in New York, on the maps used for the World Champs (and uniformly regarded as extremely high quality). Your chance to live and breath orienteering for a full two weeks.

From The Tales Of Sacajawea

by Elis Eberlein

In this issue you can find the invitation to participate in the first ever B-meet in SE Washington and organized by SACO. We will be using our two largest maps for this event. Unfortunately, those maps are outside Walla Walla and Richland, respectively, i.e. more than one hour of driving apart. I hope everyone will consider it worth the trip to come here and try our terrain. However, at this point we have to send out a warning. This is due to the late flooding in our area, and both the maps were affected by this. The "Mill Creek Project" (Saturday's map) was created to prevent flooding of downtown Walla Walla. The US Army Corps of Engineers built a diversion dam and canal and a dike that creates a lake, Bennington Lake. When Mill Creek floods, the water is diverted into Bennington Lake. When the recent flood hit the area, the lake was filled up to the edge of the dike which has never happened before. Bennington Lake is in the middle of our map and we plan to use the parking lot at the lake for the meet. When the lake was full, the level is about 20 meters above normal. This means that about half the useful part of the map was under water and the parking lot was under 6 meters of water. Fortunately, the Corps built a return pumping system as well. They estimate they could get back to normal level in about 4 weeks (mid March). If that is so, Frank and I can revise the map and set the courses in time for the meet.

The Horn Rapids map is next to the Yakima River which also flooded at very near record level. For those who participated in our December meet when we had a minor flooding, this time the water was 4-5' higher. This latest flooding will not create any problems for the meet. I will revise the map shortly, and I hope it has cleaned up some of the brush and tumbleweed that had accumulated in the gullies. I will keep you posted on the NW-net on the progress of the terrain improvement in Walla Walla.

Now to something totally different. John Beck and I have for the last couple of months been evaluating the possibility to have the Intercollegiate Championships in April of 1997. We were looking at areas S of Moses Lake. The most promising area is definitely the area S of the Potholes reservoir. This area consists of about 50 square km of terrain. Columbia National Wildlife Refuge (CNWR) manage more than half of it and the state Fish and Wildlife manage piece in the middle. CNWR will let us use minor pieces of land next to the state land, but not until August. Hunting season starts October 1. So this is a 2 month deal for not much land. The state land has no time restrictions. To have a meet in April, only state land has to be used. The area is large enough, but the access for a larger meet is practically restricted to one road. The terrain from this road is limited to a narrow cliff-covered canyon. I deemed that unsatisfactory for the IC meet. This area should definitely be mapped for some other fall event. Use of the CNWR land makes access a lot easier.

The other area I looked at is the sand dune area on the peninsula that stretches out into Potholes Reservoir from the N. The southernmost part of those dunes is a nice little area of 10 square km, that probably should be mapped. But these dunes are not like my favorite Juniper Dunes, and thus this is not IC class terrain. This is nice terrain to teach an intermediate orienteer how to read contours, but to let Eric out there is a joke! So this leaves us still without a suitable area for the IC, and time is running out to get the mapping done.

Field Notes

  • Some of you who went to the US Championship meet in June 1994 in Anchorage, Alaska might remember Ted & Ginny Moore. Ted was here a few weeks ago to have a growth removed from a sinus cavity pressing onto his eye. He and Ginny had dinner with Anne & Harvey a week after the surgery and shared tales and photos of the Moores' recent vacation to New Zealand including "West Island". While hiking in NZ, she broke her ankle and had to carried out; she still had to use crutches on her Seattle visit. They both enjoyed what they had seen of Seattle and stated that the weather in Anchorage this winter was more like Seattle weather, mild, no snow. I see from recent weather reports that indeed Anchorage weather is again cold and snowy so folks can ski again this year. Their home must really look different covered with snow.

  • Fred Veler has been named the new U.S. Team coach. Fred has already been instrumental in training for local U.S. Team member Eric Bone; now he'll have the opportunity to help the rest of the team as well. Fred has extensive orienteering experience for many years around the globe, and is well known in the northwest. Congratulations!

(Page 3)

  • Sammamish's two-day event at Bullfrog Flats has been rescheduled to June 15-16. The previous date (May 18-19) conflicted with the Sage Stomp in Kamloops. This way we can attend both events, and double our orienteering.

  • COC's two-day meet in June at Cle Elum is likely to consist of one traditional day, and one day being either a short course or relay and serving as the '96 Pig War Washington State venue. COC is still looking for meet director and course setter for this event; call Margie or Ron if you're interested.

  • The next WSOA meeting will be Sat., Jun 1 at 4pm at Kent Verbeck's house, following the Cle Elum Ridge event.

  • Fred Veler describes the May 11 Greenlake event as "map-less orienteering." It sounds like a contradiction in terms, but Fred explains that the event is fun training exercise which hones your ability to run fast while also looking for the marker in the terrain. 20 markers will be along the course around Greenlake; runners will know the codes on the marker and their order, but have no other information as to their location. Competitors have to find the markers by carefully watching the terrain as they round the lake. Since the order is known, competitors will know if they miss a marker and have to go back and look. The event will also be a fund-raiser for the U.S. Junior Team. Sounds interesting!

(Page 4)

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and the new Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

What equipment do I need?

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for rent at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Event Fees

Event fees are charged on a per-registration basis: two people running together with the same map and punch card pay a single fee. Additional maps are available; the charge varies depending on the map. Fees vary, but the following are typical fees for local ("C") events with color maps. "A" and "B" meets, state championships, rogaines, and other events will cost somewhat more.

Orienteering: 1996 Sacajawea B-meet

April 27-28 at Walla Walla and Richland, WA

Maps and terrain:

  • SATURDAY: "Mill Creek Project", 1: 10 000, 3 m contours; open grass terrain with rolling hills and some planted vegetation.
  • SUNDAY: "Horn Rapids County Park", 1:15 000, 3 m contours; flat, open grass and steppe-scrub terrain.
  • The maps were field worked by Frank Skorina (Sat) and Elis Eberlein (Sat & Sun) and drafted (OCAD) by Elis Eberlein. Computer Color Printing was used for reproduction.

Courses: 5 courses each day designed by Frank Skorina (Saturday) and Michel Billaux (Sunday) and vetted by Elis Eberlein.

Start times:

  • Saturday: noon - 3 pm
  • Sunday: 9 am - noon.

Directions:

  • Saturday: Follow signs from Hwy 12 just E of Walla Walla to Bennington Lake's Parking area.
  • Sunday: Follow signs from Hwy 240, 10 miles from the "Bypass" Hwy, NW of Richland, or from Hwy 225, 10 miles N of Benton City.

Fees: $5.00 per day for all courses. Please make checks payable to Sacajawea Orienteers in U.S. funds.

Registration: If your entry is received by April 23, you will get a premarked map and an assigned start time. Please indicate your start time requests on the entry form. If your entry is received after that day, you must mark your own map as part of the competition time. There are no additional late fees.

Rambo VII

January 20, 1996 | Point Defiance Park
Nisqually Orienteers

RAMBO VII was wet and wild this year with more rain, wind and cold temperatures than in the past. RAMBO VII was the third O-Event to use the new Pt. Defiance map. The new map features better contour and vegetation boundary definitions allowing many interesting new control points this year. The new map did not stop me from putting a little spice into RAMBO with a few unmapped features, a spot or two off the map and a few creatively named controls.
The weather, number of controls (43), and a few difficult entries combined to provide hours (three) of Orienteering fun for all. Twenty-nine of the 48 teams and individuals in this year's RAMBO scrambled to the finish with less than ten minutes on the official clock.

RAMBO highlights this year include:

  • The top Rambo team of David Enger and Jan Urban, tantalizingly close to a perfect score of 3000 points, was forced to abandoned their search for a control in "No Mans Land" as time was running out. They missed only the one control scoring an amazing 2900 points.
  • The top individual Rambo, Mike Miller, bashed his way to 2840 points.
  • The top mixed team of Eileen and Rick Breseman finished with an incredible 2760 points.
  • The top high school Rambo team of Jonathan Gingras, Peter Rutter and Ebon Kim finish with an outstanding score of 1380 points.
  • The top high school Rambo girl's team of Joan Maza and Brigitte Estvanik finished well up the competitors list with 1200 points.
  • The Bremerton team only competed for two hours. (The bus had to be back by two o'clock.)
  • As the meet director, I made the decision to correct all finish times because the official clock was not running well in the cold wet weather. After the time correction no penalty points were taken from any team. (Next year a Moore reliable timing method will be in use.)

I would like to thank Carl Moore, Linda Moore and Ken Peters for their help picking up the controls. I hope everyone had a good time and will be back again next year with friends.
-- Steve Miner

Winter O' Series

The fourth annual Winter O' Series provided much interest this year. In the men's division, it was anyone's game, as there were 5 different winners for the 5 meets. US Team member Eric Bone took the early lead with his win at Ft. Steilacoom Park. Fellow US Team member Paul Magnus then tied the series with a win at Marymoor Park a week later. The following week at Carkeek Park, Bone set the freestyle course, which, to the astonishment of all, was won by aging Brit Dave Tallent. At Bridle Trails, it was Mark Howlett's turn to win. Going into the final at Seward Park, only Bone, Howlett, and Tallent had a chance to win the overall title. In order to take the title, Tallent would have to win at Seward, Howlett could finish either 1st, 2nd, or 3rd, and Bone (since he did not run in the final event) would have to hope that neither of the other two achieved these goals. As it happened, Mike Miller spoiled everyone's plans, beating Tallent by over 1 minute. Howlett nearly finished out of the medals by mistakenly going to the wrong control partway through the course. The course consisted of two loops of the park, with many connecting lines crossing each other, making it confusing, especially at top running speed, to tell which was the next marker. However, Howlett discovered his mistake and retraced his steps, taking all the controls in order, and still finished third; a tribute to his raw running speed. The final score was Howlett 287, Bone 287, Tallent 285; the point tie between Bone and Howlett being decided by the final race result.

In the women's division, Debbie Newell made it four in a row, winning 3 of the 5 meets for a perfect score of 300. Second-place finisher Eileen Breseman, who won 2 events, was denied the opportunity to win the title, as she was on vacation in Mexico at the time of the final. Jana Urbanova, who set courses at Bridle Trails, finished in third place.

What is USOF?

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!

Yes, the Winter Series did come to a close only a few weeks ago, but the spring and summer main season will be upon us before you know it. Although there are no A-class events in Washington State this year, there are lots of 2-days in Washington and BC (including a stretch of five 2-day events on five consecutive weekends!), plus two major multi-days in the US. I won't give a detailed description of everything, but here's a brief rundown of what you can expect.

If you've never been to a two-day or multi-day, the camaraderie after the day's event is done, sitting around the campfire (or bar), arguing over the map, is a huge part of what makes these events so much fun. No matter what your skill level, try a 2-day or multi-day this summer, and see what real orienteering is like.

Many events require pre-registration, so plan ahead. There are probably several locals attending each one of these events, so call your club officers, or e-mail orienteering@u.washington.edu and you can surely find someone to share a ride and/or accommodations with. Here's the run-down of what's planned:
April 27/28. Two-day B in the Walla Walla/Richmond area. Sacajawea's first two-day event.
May 18/19. Sage Stomp A-event in Kamloops.
May 25/26. Island B-event at Newcastle Island.
June 1/2. Cle Elum Ridge. Probably one long day and a short day or relay.
June 8/9. Island B-event at Saltspring Island.
June 15/16. Bullfrog Flats two-day in Cle Elum. No word on the format yet.
June 29/30. Sage ROGAINE. I believe this is a full 24-hour ROGAINE (extended backcountry orienteering; 2-person teams).
July 4-14. Colorado 7-day. Confusingly, this has 8 days of orienteering this year. A premier event on first-class maps, with a friendly, low-key atmosphere. Most people camp out for the week.

July 20/21. Inland Empire Cup, Riverside State Park, Spokane. The legend continues! Fun, fast running, always superbly organized, and a gorgeous campground (with showers) right on the map. It just doesn't get any better than this.
July 26-28. BC Summer Games. I don't know if this is open to non-Canadians.
August 10-11. Bullfrog Flats "B". Another "B" event on this fine pair of maps.

August 10-25. The premier North American meet of the year for sure. Actually two events; a Canadian five-day in Ontario/Quebec, followed by a U.S. six-day in New York, on the maps used for the World Champs (and uniformly regarded as extremely high quality). Your chance to live and breath orienteering for a full two weeks.

In this issue you can find the invitation to participate in the first ever B-meet in SE Washington and organized by SACO. We will be using our two largest maps for this event. Unfortunately, those maps are outside Walla Walla and Richland, respectively, i.e. more than one hour of driving apart. I hope everyone will consider it worth the trip to come here and try our terrain. However, at this point we have to send out a warning. This is due to the late flooding in our area, and both the maps were affected by this. The "Mill Creek Project" (Saturday's map) was created to prevent flooding of downtown Walla Walla. The US Army Corps of Engineers built a diversion dam and canal and a dike that creates a lake, Bennington Lake. When Mill Creek floods, the water is diverted into Bennington Lake. When the recent flood hit the area, the lake was filled up to the edge of the dike which has never happened before. Bennington Lake is in the middle of our map and we plan to use the parking lot at the lake for the meet. When the lake was full, the level is about 20 meters above normal. This means that about half the useful part of the map was under water and the parking lot was under 6 meters of water. Fortunately, the Corps built a return pumping system as well. They estimate they could get back to normal level in about 4 weeks (mid March). If that is so, Frank and I can revise the map and set the courses in time for the meet.

The Horn Rapids map is next to the Yakima River which also flooded at very near record level. For those who participated in our December meet when we had a minor flooding, this time the water was 4-5' higher. This latest flooding will not create any problems for the meet. I will revise the map shortly, and I hope it has cleaned up some of the brush and tumbleweed that had accumulated in the gullies. I will keep you posted on the NW-net on the progress of the terrain improvement in Walla Walla.

Now to something totally different. John Beck and I have for the last couple of months been evaluating the possibility to have the Intercollegiate Championships in April of 1997. We were looking at areas S of Moses Lake. The most promising area is definitely the area S of the Potholes reservoir. This area consists of about 50 square km of terrain. Columbia National Wildlife Refuge (CNWR) manage more than half of it and the state Fish and Wildlife manage piece in the middle. CNWR will let us use minor pieces of land next to the state land, but not until August. Hunting season starts October 1. So this is a 2 month deal for not much land. The state land has no time restrictions. To have a meet in April, only state land has to be used. The area is large enough, but the access for a larger meet is practically restricted to one road. The terrain from this road is limited to a narrow cliff-covered canyon. I deemed that unsatisfactory for the IC meet. This area should definitely be mapped for some other fall event. Use of the CNWR land makes access a lot easier.

The other area I looked at is the sand dune area on the peninsula that stretches out into Potholes Reservoir from the N. The southernmost part of those dunes is a nice little area of 10 square km, that probably should be mapped. But these dunes are not like my favorite Juniper Dunes, and thus this is not IC class terrain. This is nice terrain to teach an intermediate orienteer how to read contours, but to let Eric out there is a joke! So this leaves us still without a suitable area for the IC, and time is running out to get the mapping done.

Some of you who went to the US Championship meet in June 1994 in Anchorage, Alaska might remember Ted & Ginny Moore. Ted was here a few weeks ago to have a growth removed from a sinus cavity pressing onto his eye. He and Ginny had dinner with Anne & Harvey a week after the surgery and shared tales and photos of the Moores' recent vacation to New Zealand including "West Island". While hiking in NZ, she broke her ankle and had to carried out; she still had to use crutches on her Seattle visit. They both enjoyed what they had seen of Seattle and stated that the weather in Anchorage this winter was more like Seattle weather, mild, no snow. I see from recent weather reports that indeed Anchorage weather is again cold and snowy so folks can ski again this year. Their home must really look different covered with snow.

Fred Veler has been named the new U.S. Team coach. Fred has already been instrumental in training for local U.S. Team member Eric Bone; now he'll have the opportunity to help the rest of the team as well. Fred has extensive orienteering experience for many years around the globe, and is well known in the northwest. Congratulations!

Sammamish's two-day event at Bullfrog Flats has been rescheduled to June 15-16. The previous date (May 18-19) conflicted with the Sage Stomp in Kamloops. This way we can attend both events, and double our orienteering.

COC's two-day meet in June at Cle Elum is likely to consist of one traditional day, and one day being either a short course or relay and serving as the '96 Pig War Washington State venue. COC is still looking for meet director and course setter for this event; call Margie or Ron if you're interested.
The next WSOA meeting will be Sat., Jun 1 at 4pm at Kent Verbeck's house, following the Cle Elum Ridge event.

Fred Veler describes the May 11 Greenlake event as "map-less orienteering." It sounds like a contradiction in terms, but Fred explains that the event is fun training exercise which hones your ability to run fast while also looking for the marker in the terrain. 20 markers will be along the course around Greenlake; runners will know the codes on the marker and their order, but have no other information as to their location. Competitors have to find the markers by carefully watching the terrain as they round the lake. Since the order is known, competitors will know if they miss a marker and have to go back and look. The event will also be a fund-raiser for the U.S. Junior Team. Sounds interesting!

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and the new Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for rent at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

January 20, 1996 | Point Defiance Park Nisqually Orienteers

RAMBO VII was wet and wild this year with more rain, wind and cold temperatures than in the past. RAMBO VII was the third O-Event to use the new Pt. Defiance map. The new map features better contour and vegetation boundary definitions allowing many interesting new control points this year. The new map did not stop me from putting a little spice into RAMBO with a few unmapped features, a spot or two off the map and a few creatively named controls. The weather, number of controls (43), and a few difficult entries combined to provide hours (three) of Orienteering fun for all. Twenty-nine of the 48 teams and individuals in this year's RAMBO scrambled to the finish with less than ten minutes on the official clock.

The top Rambo team of David Enger and Jan Urban, tantalizingly close to a perfect score of 3000 points, was forced to abandoned their search for a control in "No Mans Land" as time was running out. They missed only the one control scoring an amazing 2900 points.
The top individual Rambo, Mike Miller, bashed his way to 2840 points.
The top mixed team of Eileen and Rick Breseman finished with an incredible 2760 points.
The top high school Rambo team of Jonathan Gingras, Peter Rutter and Ebon Kim finish with an outstanding score of 1380 points.
The top high school Rambo girl's team of Joan Maza and Brigitte Estvanik finished well up the competitors list with 1200 points.
The Bremerton team only competed for two hours. (The bus had to be back by two o'clock.)

As the meet director, I made the decision to correct all finish times because the official clock was not running well in the cold wet weather. After the time correction no penalty points were taken from any team. (Next year a Moore reliable timing method will be in use.)
I would like to thank Carl Moore, Linda Moore and Ken Peters for their help picking up the controls. I hope everyone had a good time and will be back again next year with friends. -- Steve Miner

March 3, 1996 | Cascade Orienteering Club Meet Director: Jim Siscel Courses: Jim Siscel

The fourth annual Winter O' Series provided much interest this year. In the men's division, it was anyone's game, as there were 5 different winners for the 5 meets. US Team member Eric Bone took the early lead with his win at Ft. Steilacoom Park. Fellow US Team member Paul Magnus then tied the series with a win at Marymoor Park a week later. The following week at Carkeek Park, Bone set the freestyle course, which, to the astonishment of all, was won by aging Brit Dave Tallent. At Bridle Trails, it was Mark Howlett's turn to win. Going into the final at Seward Park, only Bone, Howlett, and Tallent had a chance to win the overall title. In order to take the title, Tallent would have to win at Seward, Howlett could finish either 1st, 2nd, or 3rd, and Bone (since he did not run in the final event) would have to hope that neither of the other two achieved these goals. As it happened, Mike Miller spoiled everyone's plans, beating Tallent by over 1 minute. Howlett nearly finished out of the medals by mistakenly going to the wrong control partway through the course. The course consisted of two loops of the park, with many connecting lines crossing each other, making it confusing, especially at top running speed, to tell which was the next marker. However, Howlett discovered his mistake and retraced his steps, taking all the controls in order, and still finished third; a tribute to his raw running speed. The final score was Howlett 287, Bone 287, Tallent 285; the point tie between Bone and Howlett being decided by the final race result.

In the women's division, Debbie Newell made it four in a row, winning 3 of the 5 meets for a perfect score of 300. Second-place finisher Eileen Breseman, who won 2 events, was denied the opportunity to win the title, as she was on vacation in Mexico at the time of the final. Jana Urbanova, who set courses at Bridle Trails, finished in third place.

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!
